title: A Critique of Western Myths and India's Muscular Doctrine
content:
In 1984, the film 'Missing in Action' erupted in American theatres, starring the iconic 'one-man army' Chuck Norris. The film was a piece of Reagan-era revisionism, attempting to recast the lost cause of Vietnam as a heroic rescue mission. Norris won instant acclaim alongside other Hollywood action stars, helping audiences live out the ultimate fantasy of American invincibility. Western film aficionados made peace with the over-the-top innuendo, rarely lamenting the barely disguised advocacy of a muscular, uncompromising foreign policy. This cinematic phenomenon reflected a deep cultural desire for decisive, strength-based solutions rather than sugar-coated diplomacy.

Interestingly, a similar cultural and political shift eventually occurred on the other side of the world in India. For a long time, Delhi's approach to national security challenges was marked by what many viewed as effete and tentative responses. Even during moments of profound national trauma, the government frequently reverted to stiff diplomatese rather than overt military retaliation. When the public mood demanded a decisive response, leaders often pushed for dialogue, which critics dismissed as a weak form of engagement. This historical hesitancy created a growing appetite among the public for a more robust and assertive national posture.

A visibly muscular doctrine finally emerged under the leadership of Prime Minister Narendra Modi. Within a few years of taking office, his administration's resolve was severely tested by regional adversaries. Unlike his predecessors, Modi walked his talk, replacing cautious dialogue with decisive, forceful action. This shift marked a fundamental change in India's geopolitical strategy, aligning national policy with the public's demand for strength. Ultimately, it signaled the end of sugar-coated diplomacy in favor of a more uncompromising stance on national security.
